elements.sent-2411-001: The first-order Lévy-Leblond differential equations (LLEs) are the non-relativistic analogous of the Dirac equation: they are the “square roots” of the Schrödinger equation in ($1+d$) dimensions and admit spinor solutions.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper we show how to extend to the Lévy-Leblond spinors the real/complex/quaternionic classification of the relativistic spinors (which leads to the notions of Dirac, Weyl, Majorana, Majorana-Weyl, Quaternionic spinors).
elements.sent-2411-003: Besides the free equations, we also consider the presence of potential terms.
elements.sent-2411-004: Applied to a conformal potential, the simplest $(1+1)$-dimensional LLE induces a new differential realization of the $osp(1|2)$ superalgebra in terms of differential operators depending on the time and space coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Dirac equation which introduces the notion of relativistic spinors can be regarded, see \cite{dir , as a ``square root equation\cc ~of the relativistic Klein-Gordon equation.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In a similar spirit, L\'evy-Leblond introduced in 1967 a $1+3$-dimensional equation \cite{lle which is the square root of the nonrelativistic (invariant under the Galilei group) Schr\"odinger equation.}
elements.sent-2411-007: The equation implies the presence of nonrelativistic L\'evy-Leblond spinors.
elements.sent-2411-008: Quite often the L\'evy-Leblond equation (``LLE\cc ~for short) appears when taking, for the speed of light $c$ of a relativistic system, the $c\rightarrow \infty$ limit.
elements.sent-2411-009: On the other hand, LLE has its own virtue; it can be applied to investigate nonrelativistic systems, as those studied in condensed matter, which do not necessarily arise as a limit of relativistic systems.
elements.sent-2411-010: Relativistic equations can be generalized to arbitrary $(s,t)$ spacetimes, not only the $1+3$ Minkowski spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-011: Depending on the spacetime, different solutions can be found.
elements.sent-2411-012: Besides complex spinors (as the original Dirac spinors), real Majorana spinors can be found, Weyl chiral/antichiral spinors can exist which, in certain spacetimes, can be combined to give Majorana-Weyl spinors.
elements.sent-2411-013: Spinors defined for the division algebra of quaternions produce different types of relativistic equations.
elements.sent-2411-014: The classification of the relativistic spinors is based on the \cite{abs classification of the associated Clifford algebras.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Relevant works (for this current paper) which present the classification of relativistic spinors are \cite{{kugtow ,{oku ,{crt .\par Generalized L\'evy-Leblond equations can be introduced as square roots of Schr\"odinger equations in $1+d$-dimensions, where the number $d$ of the space components is an arbitrary integer.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The Schr\"odinger equations under consideration can be free, but can also admit potential terms.
elements.sent-2411-017: The theories of LLEs and their associated spinors have not been systematically investigated as their relativistic counterparts (see, e.g., \cite{aaca ).\par Some words should be added to justify the interest in classifying the theories of nonrelativistic spinors.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Obviously, information about relativistic theories should be recovered in the $c\rightarrow \infty$ nonrelativistic theory and the mapping between the relativistic spinors and their nonrelativistic counterparts is an essential tool.
elements.sent-2411-019: Similarly, in recent years, the opposite $c\rightarrow 0$ (so-called ``Carroll\cc ) limit of relativistic theories is widely investigated (the connection between Galilei and Carroll limits is discussed in \cite{fig ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: The Galilei algebra is not semisimple and admits the Bargmann's central extension \cite{bar ; due to that its representation theory is quite nontrivial.}
elements.sent-2411-021: It also implies, as shown below in Section {\bf 5 , a different role for the time and for the space coordinates.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Last but not least, in applications to condensed matter emergent nonrelativistic spinors can arise in theories which do not necessarily result from the $c\rightarrow \infty$ limit of relativistic models.\par In this paper we show how to extend, to the generalized LLEs, the methods presented in \cite{oku and \cite{crt for relativistic spinors.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: It must be said that LLEs turned out to have surprisingly rich structures.
elements.sent-2411-024: The dynamical symmetry of the free equations possess \cite{{aktt1 ,{aktt2 $Z_2\times Z_2$-graded Lie superalgebra structures, fitting the class of superalgebras introduced by Rittenberg-Wyler in \cite{{rw1 ,{rw2 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Furthermore it was recently shown \cite{ryan that, in the presence of a harmonic potential, the $Z_2\times Z_2$-graded Lie superalgebras act as spectrum-generating superalgebras.\par In this paper we show how to systematically classify the $1+d$-dimensional L\'evy-Leblond spinors as real (that is, of Majorana-type), complex (Dirac-type), quaternionic, chiral/antichiral (Weyl-type) and in determining their vector dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We proceed at first with the free equations; later we show how to introduce potentials.
elements.sent-2411-027: The specialization to the inverse square potentials of the Conformal mechanics \cite{cal implies new differential realizations of the superconformal algebras ($osp(1|2)$ in the simplest case) in terms of differential operators in the time and space coordinates.\par As a technical tool we introduce the convenient \cite{tove ``alphabetic presentation\cc ~of Clifford algebras which allows to represent the four $2\times 2$ building-block matrices of \cite{crt as letters and the generic Clifford algebras gamma-matrices as words in a $4$-letter alphabet.}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The Clifford algebra $Cl(p,q)$ is the enveloping algebra produced by the $n\times n$ matrices $\gamma_i$ which satisfy, for $i,j= 1,2,\ldots, p+q$, the anticommutation relations \bea \{\gamma_i,\gamma_j\} = 2\eta_{ij {\mathbb I _{n\times n , \eea where ${\mathbb I _{n\times n}$ is the Identity matrix and $\eta_{ij}$ is a $(p+q)\times (p+q)$ diagonal matrix with $p$ entries $+1$ and $q$ entries $-1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In application to relativistic theories $p$ is the number of space-like coordinates and $q$ is the number of time-like coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-030: The irreducible representations of the $Cl(p,q)$ algebras are recovered \cite{{oku ,{crt from tensoring four $2\times 2$ real matrices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: They can be identified with four letters according to { { \bea X= \left(\begin{array {cccc 1 0 0 -1 \end{array}\right),\quad Y= \left(\begin{array {cccc 0 1 1 0 \end{array}\right),\quad A= \left(\begin{array {cccc 0 1 -1 0 \end{array}\right),\quad I= \left(\begin{array {cccc 1 0 0 1 \end{array}\right). \eea By dropping the symbol ``$\otimes$\cc ~in the tensor products expressing the generic $\gamma_i$ matrices entering (\ref{gammamat ), we can associate these gamma matrices with words written in a $4$-letter alphabet, see \cite{tove for details.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The three single-letter words $X,Y,A$ define the three gamma matrices entering the $Cl(2,1)$ Clifford algebra.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the \cite{crt recursive construction the five $4\times 4$ gamma matrices defining $Cl(3,2)$ can be expressed as the two-letter words $XX, XY, XA, YI, AI$ (the three matrices $XX,XY,YI$ are space-like, while the two matrices $XA,AI$ are time-like).}
elements.sent-2411-034: The general construction is presented in \cite{crt .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The possibility of having Weyl-type spinors is ensured if all gamma matrices are of block-antidiagonal form.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the alphabetic presentation this requirement implies that all corresponding words should start with an $Y$ or $A$ letter.\par The ${\mathbb R /{\mathbb C /{\mathbb H}$ real/complex/quaternionic structures of the Clifford algebras and of their associated relativistic spinors are obtained, via Schur's lemma, by looking at the most general matrices which, at given $p,q$, commute with all gamma matrices: $\bullet$ in the ${\mathbb R}$ case the most general matrix is proportional to the identity ${\mathbb I _{n\times n}$, $\bullet$ in the ${\mathbb C}$ case it is given, for $a,b$ reals, by $a{\mathbb I _{n\times n +b J$, where $J^2=-{\mathbb I _{n\times n}$, $\bullet$ in the ${\mathbb H}$ case it is given, for $a,b_1,b_2,b_3$ reals, by $a{\mathbb I _{n\times n +\sum_i b_i J_i$, where the three matrices $J_i$ are imaginary quaternions satisfying the equations $J_iJ_j=-\delta_{ij {\mathbb I _{n\times n +\varepsilon_{ijk J_k$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The three above cases find an adequate description in terms of the alphabetic presentation.\par ~ The extension of the classification to the L\'evy-Leblond nonrelativistic spinors requires introducing, in the alphabetic presentation, a ``fifth letter\cc ~denoted by $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-038: It correspond to the $2\times 2$ differential matrix { {\bea Q= \left(\begin{array {cc 0 1 i\partial_{t 0 \end{array}\right) \Rightarrow Q^2 = i\partial_{t \left(\begin{array {cc 1 0 0 1 \end{array}\right).}}}}}}}}
elements.section-2411-003: L\'evy-Leblond free equations and types of spinors
elements.sent-2411-039: By setting $\hbar=1$ and $m=\frac{1 {2}$ the free (matrix) Schr\"odinger equation in $1+d$ dimensions reads as \bea i\hbar\partial_t\Psi(t,{\vec x )= -\frac{{\vec{\nabla ^2 {2m \Psi(t,{\vec x ) \Rightarrow i\partial_t \Psi(t,{\vec x ) = -{\vec{\nabla ^2\Psi(t,{\vec x ), \eea where $\Psi(t,{\vec x )$ is an $n$-component vector (for $n=1,2,3,4,\ldots$).}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Its simplest square root equation is obtained for $d=1$ and $n=2$.
elements.sent-2411-041: By using the (\ref{4letters ,\ref{qmatrix ) notation it is written as \bea Q\Psi(t,x)= X\partial_x\Psi(t,x) \Rightarrow i\partial_t\Psi=Q^2\Psi = QX\partial_x\Psi = -X\partial_x Q\Psi= -X^2\partial_x^2\Psi =-\partial_x^2\Psi.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The next simplest case is the square root of the $n=4$-components $d=2$ free Schr\"odinger equation with space coordinates $x,y$.
elements.sent-2411-043: By taking an alphabetic basis of the five gamma matrices of the Clifford algebra $Cl(3,2)$, given by $XX, XY, XA, YI, AI$, one can express the square root equation as \bea QI\Psi = (XX\partial_x+XY\partial_y)\Psi.
elements.sent-2411-044: Due to the chirality constraint its fundamental components, in real counting, are $\frac{1 {2}\times 4 =2$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The building blocks are the gamma matrices of the $Cl(4,3)$ Clifford algebras.
elements.sent-2411-046: Two equivalent alphabetic presentations are given by the following two sets of seven $3$-letter words.
elements.sent-2411-047: The first set is given by $XXX, XXY, XXA, XYI, XAI, YII, AII$.
elements.sent-2411-048: The second set by $XYX, XYY, XYA, XXI, XAI, YII, AII$.\par Three inequivalent square root equations are obtained.
elements.sent-2411-049: The first one is \bea QII \Psi = (XXX\partial_x+XXY\partial_y+XYI\partial_z)\Psi, \eea producing $8$-component Majorana-type spinors for a $1+3$-dimensional free LLE (the space coordinates being $x,y,z$).
elements.sent-2411-050: The second equation is \bea QII \Psi = XYI\partial_x\Psi.
elements.sent-2411-052: The equation (\ref{eq3 ) is an $8$-component $(1+2)$-dimensional free LLE.\par ~\par The next level is obtained for $n=16$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: At this level one recovers $5$ inequivalent free L\'evy-Leblond equations.
elements.sent-2411-054: They are: ~ $\bullet$ the $16$-component ``Majorana-type\cc ~spinor for the $(1+4)$-dimensional free LLE, given by \bea QIII\Psi = (XXXX\partial_x+XXXY\partial_y+XXYI\partial_z+XYII\partial_w)\Psi; \eea $\bullet$ the ``Dirac-type\cc ~complex spinor for the $(1+2)$-dimensional free LLE with $IIIA$ complex structure, \bea QIII\Psi = (XXYI\partial_x+XYII\partial_y)\Psi; \eea $\bullet$ the ``Majorana-Weyl-type\cc ~spinor (with $8$ real components) for the $(1+3)$-dimensional free LLE \bea QYII\Psi = (XYXX\partial_x+XYXY\partial_y+XYYI\partial_z)\Psi; \eea $\bullet$ the ``Weyl-type\cc ~complex spinor for the $(1+1)$-dimensional free LLE with $IIIA$ complex structure, \bea QYII\Psi = XYYI\partial_x\Psi; \eea $\bullet$ the quaternionic spinor for the $(1+1)$-dimensional free LLE with $IIIA, IIAX, IIAY$ quaternionic structure, \bea QIII\Psi = XYII\partial_x\Psi.
elements.sent-2411-055: The construction can be easily extended to any $k$.
elements.sent-2411-056: It is based on the properties of the associated $Cl(p,q)$ Clifford algebras.
elements.sent-2411-057: Up to $k\leq 4$ we can present the following table illustrating the Majorana-type ($M$), Weyl-type ($W$), Majorana-Weyl-type ($MW$), Dirac-type ($D$), Quaternionic ($H$) nonrelativistic spinors of the free $(1+d)$-dimensional LLEs.

elements.sent-2411-059: In this Section we show how our scheme can be enlarged to introduce L\'evy-Leblond equations which are square roots of matrix Schr\"odinger equations in the presence of potential terms.
elements.sent-2411-060: We illustrate the simplest example of the nonrelativistic Majorana spinors in $1+1$ dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-061: While the free L\'evy-Leblond equation in $1+1$ dimensions can be realized by $2\times 2$ matrices, we need at least $4\times 4$ matrices to include the potentials.
elements.sent-2411-063: The basic equation is \bea QI\Psi = XY\partial_{x}\Psi +XAf(x)\Psi, \eea where the ``words" $QI$, $XY$, and $XA$ represent $4\times 4$ matrices; the symbol $\partial_x$ denotes the derivative with respect to the space coordinate $x$ and the function $f(x)$ is the prepotential which allows to reconstruct the potential terms of the matrix Schr\"odinger equation.
elements.sent-2411-064: The column vector spinor $\Psi(x,t)$ has $4$ components ($\Psi^T=(\psi_1,\psi_2,\psi_3,\psi_4$)).
elements.sent-2411-065: The following equations are satisfied for its $\psi_i(x,t)$ components with $i=1,2,3,4$: \bea \psi_{3 = \partial_{x}\psi_2+f(x)\psi_2,\nonumber \psi_{4 = \partial_{x}\psi_1-f(x)\psi_1, \eea together with the time-dependent equations \bea i\partial_{t}\psi_1 = -\partial_{x}\psi_4-f(x)\psi_4,\nonumber i\partial_{t}\psi_2 = -\partial_{x}\psi_3+f(x)\psi_3.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The simplest example is illustrated in the next Section.
elements.section-2411-005: The $osp(1|2)$ superalgebra induced by a conformal potential
elements.sent-2411-067: In $1+1$ dimensions, the L\'evy-Leblond ``square root” of a matrix conformal mechanics with potential term proportional to $1/{x^2}$ is given by the $4\times 4$ matrix differential equation \bea \Omega \Psi (x,t) = 0, \eea where the $\Psi(x,t)$ L\'evy-Leblond spinor is a $4$-component column vector and the matrix differential operator $\Omega$ belongs to a $5$-generator $osp(1|2)$ superalgebra.
elements.sent-2411-068: The explicit form of the five $osp(1|2)$ generators is given in terms of the $2\times 2$ matrices $I,X,Y,A$ and $Q$ introduced in (\ref{4letters ) and (\ref{qmatrix ), plus two extra $2\times 2$ auxiliary matrices $\Lambda$ and $R$ which depend on an (arbitrary) real scaling parameter $\lambda$: \bea \Lambda = \left(\begin{array {cc \lambda 0 0 \lambda+\frac{1 {2}\end{array}\right), R = \left(\begin{array {cc 0 0 \lambda 0\end{array}\right).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069:
The two generators $\Omega, \Xi$ are odd (fermionic); they respectively correspond to the positive and negative simple roots of $osp(1|2)$.\par The closure of the $osp(1|2)$ superalgebra is given by the following (anti)commutators:  ea elax [D,H]=-H,\qquad [D,K] = K, \qquad [H,K] =2D,

onumber elax \qquad\qquad\qquad ~~ [H,\Omega]=0,\qquad\quad [K,\Omega] = -\Xi,\qquad [K,\Omega]= - \Xi,\quad [K,\Xi]=0, 
onumber \{\Omega,\Omega\}= 2H,\qquad \{\Omega,\Xi\} = 2D,\qquad \{\Xi,\Xi \}= 2 K. \eea Therefore, $\Omega$ is the square root of the $4	imes 4$ matrix conformal mechanics Schr"odinger equation  ea H\Psi(x,t) = 0.}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: For the free Schr\"odinger equation, a second-order differential realization of $osp(1|2)$ in terms of the space coordinate $x$ appears naturally (see \cite{duality , which connects the \cite{wig Wigner's approach to the quantization of the harmonic oscillator with the Niederer's analysis \cite{{nie1 ,{nie2 ,{nie3 of the maximal kinematical invariance group of the Schr\"odinger equation for various potentials).\par The L\'evy-Leblond equation $\Omega\Psi=0$ induces, for the conformal potential proportional to $\frac{1 {x^2}$, a new interesting and non-trivial differential realization of $osp(1|2)$ which involves both time and space coordinates.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The addition in conformal mechanics of a harmonic potential term to the $\frac{1 {x^2}$ potential produces the de Alfaro-Fubini-Furlan deformed oscillator \cite{{dff ,{hoto having the same conformal algebra as spectrum-generating algebra.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The new Hamiltonian is the sum of ${\bf H}$ and of its conformal partner.
elements.sent-2411-073: Concerning the (\ref{osp12 ) realization, one should note that the sum $H+K$ does not produce a L\'evy-Leblond equation.}
elements.sent-2411-074: It is an open question how to produce a de Alfaro-Fubini-Furlan construction for the LLE.
elements.sent-2411-075: This paper outlines the classification of the L\'evy-Leblond nonrelativistic spinors and of their associated equations using tools and methods borrowed from the classification of relativistic spinors.
elements.sent-2411-076: It further illustrates in simple examples how to introduce potential terms to L\'evy-Leblond equations and how to derive the ``square root\cc ~of conformal mechanics with their induced superconformal algebras.
elements.sent-2411-077: This work is part of a research program where an extended version and systematic presentation of the constructions for real/complex/quaternionic and Weyl-type L\'evy-Leblond spinors is under preparation.
